coredata.dc:title: Oxidation anchoring strategy for the retention of nanostructures in high temperature carbonization process to enhance hard carbon sodium storage performance

coredata.dc:description: The combination of defect-rich nanostructures with hard carbon is an effective way to increase the slope capacity for improving the electrochemical sodium storage performance, however, the difficulty of preparing nanostructures and self-agglomerating of nanocomponent seriously hinders the further application of this method. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CDs) anchoring in hard carbon (HC) have been successfully prepared through simple crosslinking, peroxidation and carbonization. The preoxidation effectively anchors CDs in the carbon precursor, thus avoiding self-aggregation at high temperature. The introduction of CDs rich in edge defects makes HC/CDs possess a large number of adsorption sites of sodium ion, and the crosslinking of carbon precursors promotes the curling and entanglement of graphite microdomains in hard carbon, resulting in the expansion of layer spacing and the development of closed pores, which is conducive to the intercalation and pore filling of sodium ion. Profiting from the above optimization, HC/CDs-3 successfully achieved a collaborative majorization in platform and slope capacity, and its electrochemical performance was significantly improved in terms of specific capacity, rate performance and cycle stability.

head.ce:title: Oxidation anchoring strategy for the retention of nanostructures in high temperature carbonization process to enhance hard carbon sodium storage performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of defect-rich nanostructures with hard carbon is an effective way to increase the slope capacity for improving the electrochemical sodium storage performance, however, the difficulty of preparing nanostructures and self-agglomerating of nanocomponent seriously hinders the further application of this method. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CDs) anchoring in hard carbon (HC) have been successfully prepared through simple crosslinking, peroxidation and carbonization. The preoxidation effectively anchors CDs in the carbon precursor, thus avoiding self-aggregation at high temperature. The introduction of CDs rich in edge defects makes HC/CDs possess a large number of adsorption sites of sodium ion, and the crosslinking of carbon precursors promotes the curling and entanglement of graphite microdomains in hard carbon, resulting in the expansion of layer spacing and the development of closed pores, which is conducive to the intercalation and pore filling of sodium ion. Profiting from the above optimization, HC/CDs-3 successfully achieved a collaborative majorization in platform and slope capacity, and its electrochemical performance was significantly improved in terms of specific capacity, rate performance and cycle stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the limited availability of lithium resources and the increasing need for extensive energy storage devices, there is a shift towards sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) as a replacement for lithium-ion batteries, which is driven by the economic benefits and sustainable use of raw materials[1–3]. Despite the similarities in physical and chemical properties between Na and Li, the larger ionic radius and the thermodynamic mismatch in graphite layers of Na+ render traditional graphite anode unsuitable for SIBs[4,5]. In contrast to the regular structure of graphite, hard carbon, which is composed of bent, twisted and wound graphite domains, has become the preferred anode material for SIBs[6]. The abundant surface defects, wide layer spacing and a large number of closed pores of hard carbon determine the adsorption-insertion-filling sodium storage behaviour[7,8].

In the adsorption-insertion-filling mechanism, adsorption and insertion-fill are often considered to correspond to a high voltage slope region and a low voltage platform range, respectively[9–11]. Currently, platform capacity, an important factor affecting the energy density of the whole battery has become the focus of research. However, excessive platform capacity controlled by diffusion behavior could affect the magnification performance of SIB, and the lower average voltage makes it easy to precipitate metal sodium resulting in safety hazards[12,13]. On the contrary, the surface-controlled slope capacity shows rapid sodium storage capacity, so appropriately increasing the slope capacity of hard carbon while improving the platform capacity is the key to SIB further entering the market.

The introduction of functional groups or defects is an effective means to improve slope capacity[14]. However, the mass of the former will lead to high surface energy and structural instability, resulting in rapid capacity attenuation during the cycle[15]. The latter becomes the optimal solution to increase the capacity of the slope region. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, with rich heteroatomic functional groups, edge defects and unique quantum confinement effects, have been the first choice for defect-rich vectors and widely used in energy storage and conversion materials[16–18]. For example, Zhao et al.[19] combined CDs with reduced graphene oxide by hydrothermal method to augment the surface Na+ storage sites. Nevertheless, the out-of-situ combination method is limited by the surface structure of the carrier and the existence environment of CDs, seriously affecting the homogeneity of the materials. Guo et al.[20] prepared CDs in-situ composited hard carbon through pyrolysis cellulose acetate butyrate at 200 °C and carbonization at 700 °C achieving high adsorption of K+. The low carbonization temperature led to insufficient development of graphite domain and closed pore severely impairing the insertion and filling of Na+, however, the nanostructure of CDs would agglomerate due to the high specific surface energy at the carbonization temperature destroying nanostructures[21]. How to efficiently introduce CDs into hard carbon is an urgent problem at present.

In this work, the lignin and divinylbenzene were crosslinked by Carbonyl-Ene reaction and CDs were in-situ generated in the crosslinked lignin. The CDs composited hard carbon materials (HC/CDs) were obtained by high temperature carbonization and the self-aggregation of CDs during carbonization was avoided by oxidation anchoring strategy. The appropriate precursor crosslinking could regulate the layer spacing and closed pores to promote the platform capacity, and the introduction of CDs can regulate the surface defects and opened pores to promote the slope capacity. Owing to the aforementioned optimizations, the electrochemical performance of HC/CDs including specific capacity, rate capability, and cycle stability, has undergone substantial improvement in comparison to unmodified hard carbon.

A commercial lignin (industrial grade) was obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Company (Shanghai, China). Divinylbenzene (DVB, 99.9 %) was produced from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Company (Tianjin, China). Argon (Ar, 99.99 %) was obtained from Taineng Gas Company (Taiyuan, China). All chemicals are analytically pure without further treatment.

Typically, 20 g of lignin and different amounts of DVB (20, 30 and 40 mL) were added to a 1-liter high-pressure reactor respectively and stirred at 280 °C for 1 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was named L/D-x, where x represented the mass of DVB. Furthermore, L/D-x powder was heated to 300 °C (1.5 °C min−1) and maintained for 2 h, which was carried out under a stream of Air (100 mL min−1). The oxidation product is named L/C-x-O.

The CDs and hard carbon composite carbon materials were obtained by high temperature carbonization. Especially, the L/D-x-O was carbonized at 1400 °C (5 °C min−1) for 2 h with a stream of Ar (100 mL min−1). The carbon material was named HC/CDs-x, where the x is the same as in L/D-x-O. Additionally, lignin and L/D-3 were carbonized at high temperature with the same conditions, named HC and HC-3 respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using the Bruker Vertex 70 instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired using a Thermo Kalpha apparatus. Thermogravimetric (TG) and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) analyses were carried out using an HCT-1 instrument in a N2 atmosphere. The XRD patterns were obtained by employing a D8 ADVANCE A25 X-ray powder diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406). The Bruker Nanostar instrument were used to collect Small-Angle X-Ray Scattering (SAXS) patterns. Ture densities were obtained by an AccuPyc II 1340 analyzer with Helium analysis gas. The Raman spectra was obtained using a Thermo Fischer DXR spectrometer. The morphologies were inspected using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). N2 and CO2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were measured with a Micromeritics TriStar II 3020 analyzer.

The electrochemical assessment of all carbon materials was carried out in CR2032 coin-type cells. To obtain the electrode, a slurry was formulated by blending hard carbon, Super P, and Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) in a ratio of 8:1:1. The slurry was subsequently coated onto a copper foil and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h. In the half-cell system, the sodium metal, glass fiber (GF/D) and 1 M NaClO4 (EC/DEC=1:1) served as the counter electrode, diaphragm and electrolyte respectively. The complete battery assembly was conducted inside a glove box with H2O and O2 concentrations lower than 0.1 ppm. The LAND CT3002 battery tester was employed to conduct Galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) and the Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration (GITT) tests. The CHI 660E electrochemical workstation was used to perform cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical resistance spectroscopy (EIS) tests. The electrochemical voltage window ranged from 0.001 to 3 V for all tests. The frequency range of EIS was 0.01–100,000 Hz. As for the full-cell tests, the HC/CDs-3 and NaFe0.33Ni0.33Mn0.33O2 were used as anode and cathode materials respectively and assembled into a full cell. The preparation of the electrode sheet, the assembly of the cell and the electrochemical test conducted on the full cell were the same as for the half cell, with the only difference being a change in the voltage window to 0–4 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure analysis of the lignin and modified lignin
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the C=O of lignin and the C=C of DVB were polymerized at high temperature to achieve the purpose of cross-linking, meanwhile, DVB was aggregated into CDs which was randomly distributed in the cross-linked lignin. After oxidation in air, crosslinked lignin and CDs were both grafted with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, and the oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs and crosslinked lignin interacted to anchor it in the crosslinked lignin, which could avoid the self-condensation of CDs in carbonization. Finally, the hard carbon combined with CDs was prepared after high temperature carbonization. By adjusting the addition of DVB, the crosslinking degree of lignin and the number of CDs could be controlled to optimize the sodium storage capacity by adjusting the microstructure of materials.

The differences in the chemical functional group between lignin, L/D-3 and L/D-x-O were demonstrated by the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 2 a. After crosslinking modification, the wide peak at 3600–3100 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of O–H functional group decreases significantly due to the introduction of numerous aromatic ring structures[22], in addition the peak at 1700 cm−1 attributed to C=O functional group directly disappears indicating that the C=C of DVB successfully reacted with the C=O of lignin[23]. Notably, the peaks at the 950–700 cm−1 range (the out-of-plane bending vibration mode of C–H on the aromatic nucleus) change from a single peak to a multi-peak segment, which is a characteristic peak of the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structure due to the introduction of CDs, and they are significantly enhanced with the increase of DVB addition[24]. After oxidation the notable augmentation of the O–H peak and the reappearance of the C=O peak indicate L/D-x-O with lots of oxygen-containing functional groups, and they are enhanced with increasing DVB owing to rich edge defects of CDs which offer numerous active sites for the grafting of additional functional groups.

The surface chemical constituents and bonding structures were determined through XPS analysis of carbon precursor, and the results are shown in Fig. 2b and Table S1. The XPS spectra exhibit distinct signal peaks for C 1 s and O 1 s. As a result of the incorporation of a substantial amount of DVB and the self-cleavage of oxygen-containing functional groups, the oxygen content of L/D-3 is reduced compared to lignin. After oxidative anchoring, the CDs and modified lignin undergo grafting with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, leading to a notable increase in oxygen content that escalates with the increase of DVB. As shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S1, the spectra are deconvoluted into four peaks including the C–C sp2 of 284.5 eV, C–C sp3 285.3 eV, C-O of 286.4, and C=O of 288.4 eV respectively[25]. Among them, the C–C sp3 and C–C sp2 correspond to the degree of disorder structure and the degree of ordered structure respectively[26]. In Fig. 2d, the elevated C=C/C–C ratio observed in L/D-3 compared to lignin is attributed to the incorporation of a significant number of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures by CDs, additionally within the L/D-x-O series, the C=C/C–C ratio increases as more CDs are introduced. Furthermore, the decrease in the C=C/C–C ratio of L/D-3-O suggests that functional groups have been successfully grafted onto the surface of CDs and cross-linked lignin leading to a reduction in aromaticity. Fig. S2 is high-resolution O 1 s spectra of L, L/D-3 and L/D-x-O which could be fitted to three peaks: carbonyl of 531.5 eV, C-O of 532.6 eV, and carboxyl of 533.7 eV[27]. The peak-splitting results are shown in Fig. 2e and the changes of carbon–oxygen bond are consistent with the infrared spectral analysis, such as the disappearance of carbonyl groups in L/D-3 after cross-linking and the re-appearance of carbonyl groups in L/D-3-O. In the L/D-x-O series, it is evident that the proportion of O-C=O serving as both crosslinking and anchoring structures increases progressively with the addition of the crosslinking agent, which underscores the effective regulation of the crosslinking degree.

To characterize the thermal stability of lignin and modified lignin, TG and DTG data were collected from room temperature to 800 °C under a N2 atmosphere (Fig. 2f). Lignin undergoes thermal degradation in the temperature range of 200 to 600 °C, due to the activity of chemical bonds and functional groups covers a wide temperature range[28,29], on the contrary the peak of lignin after crosslinking concentrates on 460 °C which is attributed to the pyrolysis of CDs and cross-linked lignin[30,31]. Although the thermal stability of L/D-3 is improved below 400 °C, the final residual carbon content of L/D-3 is lower than that of lignin due to violent decomposition and polymerization at 460 °C. In addition, L/D-3-O exhibits similar pyrolysis behavior but the peak intensity is weakened compared with L/D-3, resulting in the pyrolysis of CDs and crosslinked lignin could be effectively reduced by oxidation treatment with oxygen-containing functional groups, and the peak intensity gradually decreases with the increase of DVB addition, suggesting the addition of crosslinked structure could further stabilize the materials structure to improve the thermal stability. Finally, the residue rates of lignin, L/D-3, L/D-2-O, L/D-3-O and L/D-4-O were 37 %, 31 %, 53 %, 57 % and 58 %, respectively.

The morphology and structure of HC, HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 were observed via SEM (Fig. 3 a-c). After high temperature carbonization, HC assumes a two-dimensional sheet structure with a thickness of approximately 300 nm. Due to the significant changes in the pyrolysis behavior of crosslinked lignin, the resulting carbon materials HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 exhibit a dense block structure. The difference between HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 lies in the surface of the former distributed among a large number of particles with a diameter of about 300 nm, which is formed by the agglomeration of CDs in the carbonization. Particularly, the surface particle size of the latter is significantly reduced, indicating that grafting a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs could effectively reduce the agglomeration phenomenon. By comparing HC/CDs-x (Fig. S3), it can be found that the surface particle size increases with the addition of DVB.

High-resolution TEM observations were conducted to gain deeper insights into the microstructure of hard carbon and the characteristics of CD size and distribution. At lower magnification (Fig. 3d-f), it is evident that numerous CDs are randomly distributed across the HC/CDs-3 surface. In contrast, HC and HC-3 primarily possess the characteristics of hard carbon materials. At high magnification (Fig. 3g-i), HC exhibits a typical “house of cards” structure in that the longer graphite domains are stacked on top of each other and lattice spacing is about 0.390 nm indicating its short-range order structure[32]. Different from HC, the graphite domains in HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 are bent and twisted to form topological structures (the red dotted circle)[33], which could expand the filling capacity of the platform region. Moreover, the bending and twisting of the graphite microcrystals results in lattice distortion and the lattice spacing increases significantly (0.415 and 0.430 nm), the carbon layer with crystal face spacing greater than 0.400 nm could play a certain role in pseudo-adsorption increasing the slope capacity[34]. At the same time, CDs with a maximum diameter of 7.56 nm are randomly distributed on the surface of HC/CDs-3 which could be used as sodium ion adsorption sites to improve the absorb capacity of the slope region, and lattice spacing of CDs is 0.220 nm corresponding to the surface spacing of the (100) plane of graphene[35]. The results clearly indicate that the oxidation anchoring strategy effectively prevented the aggregation of CDs during the carbonization process and successfully facilitated the integration of CDs with hard carbon. In the HC/CDs-x series diagram (Fig. S3), similar to the SEM analysis results, CDs are distributed on all surfaces and the quantity and size of CDs increase with the increase of DVB. At the high multiples, the most clearly observed maximum sizes of CDs in HC/CDs-2, 3 and 4 are 4.75, 7.56, and 9.14 nm, respectively. In addition, CDs occur agglomeration in HC/CD-4, because they gradually aggregate to form large clusters when the number and size increase to a certain extent[36]. The lattice spacings of HC/CDs-2, 3 and 4 are 0.424, 0.430 and 0.437 nm respectively, indicating the increase of cross-linking degree helps to twist and bend of graphite microcrystal streaks during high temperature carbonization to accelerate the insertion of sodium ions.

The XRD patterns of all carbon materials (Fig. 4 a) exhibit two distinctive peaks at approximately 23° and 44°, respectively, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes of amorphous carbon[37]. D002, Lc and La calculated according to XRD are shown in Table S2. The crosslinking modification and the grafting oxygen functional groups could cause the redshift of peak (002), corresponding to the increase of crystal face spacing and consisting with the TEM analysis, owing to cross-linking contributes to the distortion of graphite microdomains and oxidation contributes to the fixation of deformed graphite microdomains[38,39]. Furthermore, the La and Lc with a downward trend indicate the formation of microcrystals is more disorderly and more closed pores are formed in their interior[40]. The increase of crystal face spacing and closed pores contributes to the increase of platform capacity.

The impact of crosslinking and oxidation on carbon crystalline are observed through the Raman spectra displayed in Fig. 4b. The two characteristic peaks are located at 1350 cm−1 (D band, related to sp3 structural defective carbon) and 1580 cm−1 (G band, related to sp2 graphitic carbon)[41]. In general, the intensity ratio of D band to G band is employed to assess the degree of disorder in carbon materials. The values of ID/IG of HC, HC-3, HC/CDs-2, 3 and 4 are 0.87, 1.10, 1.09, 1.13 and 1.14 respectively, obviously crosslinking and oxidation modification could increase the value of ID/IG, the degree of disorder of the material. Furthermore, the disappearance of the peak at 2680 cm−1 which is associated with multilayer graphene also demonstrates the increase in disorder between HC-3 and HC/CDs-x[42]. In addition, the increase of crosslinking degree prevented the crystal rearrangement of the modified lignin in the high temperature carbonization stage, meanwhile, the increase of CDs brings abundant edge active defects, which are completely consistent with the XRD analysis result.

The chemical states of elements on the surface of carbon were analyzed by XPS. All carbon materials show a similar distribution of elements in Fig. S4, and the detailed results are listed in Table S3. The oxygen content of all carbon materials is approximately 8 %, despite the presence of different oxygen content in their precursors. Many oxygen elements escape to form a stable structure of carbon materials during the carbonization stage, however, the loss of oxygen may have an impact on the surface structure of the material. In addition, the oxygen content of HC/CDs-x is slightly higher than HC and HC-3 and increases with the increase of CDs, due to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. To further understand the carbon and oxygen state the high-resolution XPS was performed. Clearly, the C 1 s spectra are fitted to four peaks corresponding to C–C sp2, C–C sp3, C-O, and C=O in Fig. S5. In Fig. 4c the C=C sp2 to C–C sp3 ratios of HC, HC-3, HC/CDs-2, 3 and 4 collected by C 1 s spectra are 1.09, 0.94, 0.93, 0.88 and 0.80, respectively, indicating that the distortion of graphite domains caused by crosslinking and oxidation and the abundant edge defects of CDs increase the proportion of defective carbon in materials, which are consistent with XRD and Raman results. In addition, the O 1 s spectra could be divided into three peaks including carbonyl groups, C-O and carboxyl groups in Fig. S6. The relative ratio of C-O bonds collected by O 1 s spectra is summarized in Fig. 4d, and the oxygen elements of HC obtained by direct carbonization of lignin are still dominated by C-O, while the proportion of carbonyl groups and carboxyl groups increases significantly in HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 and increases as the number of CDs increases indicating that CDs contain a large number of carbonyl and carboxyl groups as anchor structure.

The specific surface area and the porosity characteristics of carbon materials were obtained by The N2 adsorption/desorption measurement. In Fig. 4e, HC with a low N2 adsorption–desorption capacity under low pressure while a high N2 adsorption–desorption capacity under high pressure indicates that it contains a large number of macropore structures which belongs to Type Ⅲ isotherm [43]. Unlike HC, both HC-3 and HC/CDs-x exhibit Type Ⅳ isotherms with a distinct trailing ring, suggesting they are predominantly composed of micropores and mesopores[44]. The information about specific surface area and open pore volume are listed in Table S4. The specific surface area of HC, HC-3, HC/CDs-2, 3 and 4 are 5.4623, 0.7785, 0.8470, 0.8467 and 3.5179 m2 g−1, respectively. The specific surface area could be effectively reduced by crosslinking lignin, mainly due to the improvement of its pyrolysis behavior. In addition, the introduction of heteroatoms often leads to the increase of specific surface area due to the large number of micropores resulting from their escape at high temperatures[45], while HC/CDs-3 with a slight increase in specific surface area compared with HC-3 is attributed to the certain influence of CDs on the surface structure. With the increase of DVB addition, the specific surface area shows an increasing trend, especially for HC/CDs-4. In the carbonization stage of L/D-4-O, a large amount of oxygen element escapes and the surface CDs agglomerate due to its large number and high surface energy, which causes the structure to collapse to a certain extent, and eventually leads to a sudden increase in the specific surface area.

To elucidate the effect of crosslinking, oxidation and CDs on the evolution of porous structures, the open pores with sizes above and below 1 nm in hard carbon samples were analyzed by N2 adsorption and CO2 adsorption, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3f, the pore size distribution determined by the N2 molecule is dominated by pores of 1–100 nm. Compared with HC, the pore size of HC-3 and HC/CDs-x have decreased significantly. Interestingly, the pore of 2–8 nm size of HC/CDs-x series is close to 0 cm3 g−1 (Fig. S7), which may be due to CDs attached to the hard carbon surface covering these holes creating a directional plugging effect. In general, the pore size is inversely proportional to the specific surface area, and the 2–8 nm pore is the main force that provides the specific surface area in the mesopore, and its effective plugging could significantly improve the specific surface area that HC/CDs-3 does not significantly increase the specific surface area compared with HC-3. For pores larger than 8 nm, HC/CDs-4 showed a significant upward trend compared with HC/CDs-3, indicating that a large number of holes due to the decomposition of the crosslinker during the high temperature carbonization stage, ultimately weakening the electrochemical performance of the material. In order to analyze the pores below 1 nm, the CO2 adsorption method was used to detect hard carbon samples (Fig. S8). The pores of HC are mainly concentrated in the range of 0.45–0.60 nm, while those of HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 are concentrated in the range of 0.50–0.80 nm and 0.50–0.60 nm respectively, indicating crosslinking modification can increase the micropore size, while oxidation modification can decrease the micropore size[46].

The closed pores and topological structures formed by the bending, twisting and winding of graphite chips are particularly important for sodium ion filling[33]. As shown in Fig. S9, the wide peak at 0.1 Å−1 is a typical feature of closed pores, and the radius of the closed pore is proportional to the intensity of the peak[47]. The apparent broad peak strength and width of HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 are significantly greater than HC, and HC/CDs-3 has the strongest peak strength, indicating that the crosslinking and oxidation modification of the precursor is helpful to deformation and solidification of graphite domains to realize the development of closed pores. Subsequently, the volume of the closed pore was analyzed by the true density test according to Archimedes’ principle by Helium as the analysis gas. In Table S4, according to formula 1 and the theoretical density of graphite (2.26 cm3 g−1), the closed pore volumes of HC, HC-3 and HC/CDs-3 are calculated to be 0.0213, 0.0481 and 0.0512 cm3 g−1 respectively, which is similar to the result of SXAS.

Combined with the above results, the cross-linking of precursor and appropriate introduction of CDs cause an obvious improvement in the specific surface area, hierarchical opened pore and closed pore for HC/CDs-3.

All the obtained anode materials were assembled into half cells for electrochemical testing and analysis. The galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) test was carried out at 0.1C (30 mA g−1). The test results of the first cycle were shown in Fig. 5 a and the corresponding electrochemical performance was summarized in Table S5. All curves could be segregated into two potential regions, whereby one is the slope region (above 0.1 V) and the plateau region (below 0.1 V), which is a typical characteristic of hard carbon negative charge and discharge of SIB[48]. HC/CDs-3 exhibits the longest charge–discharge curve and a specific capacity of 368.9 mAh g-1, indicating a substantial enhancement in sodium storage capacity. The initial Coulomb efficiency (ICE) is frequently associated with the formation of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film on the material surface, closely related to surface defects and oxygen-containing functional groups of materials[49]. The ICE of HC-3 is higher than that of HC due to the successful decrease in specific surface area to reduce surface defects and oxygen-containing functional groups. However, compared to HC and HC-3, the slight reduction of ICE in HC/CDs-3 could be attributed to the tradeoff between the combination of CDs introducing many surface defects and oxygen-containing functional groups and the small specific surface area due to directional plugging, and the similar rule is observed in the HC/CDs-x series. The section on the constant-current charge–discharge curve known as the slape region is commonly associated with pseudo-capacitance characteristics, which is believed to be caused by the adsorption of sodium ions at the imperfections and interfaces of graphite-like areas, conversely, the platform region on the curve signifies battery behavior, which can be attributed to the insertion of sodium ions between layers of graphite and the occupation of closed holes[50]. To establish a correlation between the microstructure of hard carbon and its electrochemical properties, the slope and platform capacities of all samples were statistically categorized according to the charge curve, as depicted in Fig. 5b. The slope capacities of HC, HC-3, and HC/CDs-3 are 134, 139, and 160 mAh g−1, respectively. Although HC-3 contains more defects and oxygen-containing functional groups and the layer spacing of 0.400 nm compared to HC, its relatively small specific surface area leads to fewer effective adsorption sites on the outside, resulting in a small increase in its slope capacity. Furthermore, the introduction of CDs on the surface and the wider layer spacing led to a significant increase in the slope capacities of HC/CDs-3, from 139 to 160 mAh g−1, indicating that the abundant defect sites of CDs significantly improved the slope capacities of the material[51]. On the other hand, the platform capacities of HC, HC-3, and HC/CDs-3 are 150, 169, and 209 mAh g−1, respectively, due to cross-linking and oxidation promoting the formation of closed holes and the deformation of graphite chips[52]. However, a different variation pattern was observed in the HC/CD-x series. The platform and slope capacities of HC/CDs-4 are lower compared to HC/CDs-3, potentially attributed to the reduction in edge-active sites and the obstruction of numerous intercalation pathways due to extensive agglomeration of CDs within HC/CDs-4.

Fig. 5c illustrates the rate performance of all samples. At the current density of 0.2C, the capacity retention rates of HC, HC-3, HC/ CDS-2, 3 and 4 are 55.4 %, 54.3 %, 64.2 %, 64.7 % and 59.4 %, respectively. Compared with HC, the decrease in the capacity retention rate of HC-3 is attributed to the fact that its capacity growth is mainly in the platform capacity. Thanks to the increase of slope capacity, the magnification performance of HC/CDs-x is significantly improved compared with HC and HC-3[53]. Upon reducing the current density to 30 mA g−1, the anodes could restore their initial capacity suggesting the stable nature of the skeleton structures of the obtained hard carbon compositing CDs. Fig. 5d illustrates the cyclic test conducted on all samples subjected to 150 cycles at a current density of 0.1C. After 150 cycles of charge, the cycle retention rates of HC, HC-3, HC/CDs-2, HC/CDs-3, and HC/CDs-4 are recorded as 75.7 %, 76.2 %, 78.1 %, 81.7 %, and 84.3 %, respectively. Although carbon material dominated by platform capacity tends to possess poor cyclic properties[54]. In the series of HC/CDs-x, the expansion of layer spacing facilitates the long-term inserting of sodium ions, thereby enhancing the cyclic performance of the platform region, and the introduction of CDs also contributes to the stabilization of the microstructure.

The electrochemical kinetic processes were further analyzed by the electrochemical impedance test (EIS). Fig. 4e shows the Nyquist plots of all samples after 5 cycles of charge and discharge at 0.1C, which consists of semicircle and diagonal lines. The intercept between the semicircle and the horizontal coordinate in the high frequency region represents the internal resistance (Rs) of the material, the diameter of the semicircle in the middle frequency region represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the material, and the slash line in the low frequency region represents the ion diffusion resistance (Rw)[55]. The smaller Rct and Rw of HC/CDs-3 compared to HC and HC-3 are attributed to the increase of defect sites and the widening of layer spacing, indicating an optimized speed of sodium ion storage and migration. In an enlarged view of the high frequency region (Fig. S10), the incorporation of highly conductive CDs results in a lower Rs for HC/CDs-3, and the Rs decreases accordingly as the quantity of CDs increases in the HC/CDs-x series. However, the Rct and Rw decrease compared to HC/CDs-3, due to the inactivation of the CDs agglomerating active site in HC/CDs-4. The fitting results of each resistance value are shown in Table S6 (the equivalent circuit diagram is shown in Fig. S11). In Fig. 5f, compared with the hard carbon anodes recently reported, HC/CDs-3 shows better overall performance by weighing ICE and specific capacity[33,56–61].

The Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to further analyze the sodium storage kinetics of carbon materials. Figs. 6a, d and Fig. S12 show similar shapes including the sharp peak at 0–0.1 V and corresponding to the Na+ inserted into the graphite layer, and the wide peak at 0.5–0.75 V attributed to the adsorption of Na+. In addition, the intensity of all peaks of HC/CDs-3 is greater than other carbon materials indicating more intercalation sites and adsorption sites, and the largest enclosed area of HC/CDs-3 means the strongest storage capacity of sodium ions.

In order to further investigate the mechanism of higher rate performance of HC/CDs-3 than HC at different current densities, the contributions of surface- and diffusion-controlled behavior to the overall energy storage behavior of half battery were investigated by analyzing CV curves. The sharp peak at 0–0.01 V and the wide peak at 0.5–0.75 V of CV curves were calculated and fitted to obtain the b value which is the representative index of the diffusion control process (b=0.5) and surface control process (b=1) according to formulas 2 and 3, respectively. The b values of HC and HC/CDs-3 are 0.60 and 0.62 respectively, with the fitting of the sharp peak at 0–0.01 V (Fig. 6 b and e), indicating that the process is dominated by diffusion which corresponds to the process of intercalation and filling holes and HC/CDs-3 possesses faster sodium storage kinetics due to the spread of layer spacing. The fitting results of wide peaks at 0.5–0.75 V are shown in Fig. 6c and f. Compared to HC, HC/CDs-3 with a higher b value exhibit faster energy storage behavior benefiting from the elevation of slope capacity[62].

As shown in Fig. S13, HC/CDs-3 has a higher surface control contribution than HC at all scan rates and all peaks, especially at high scanning rate the surface capacity ratio can reach 60.5 % and 89.1 % at 0–0.1 V and 0.5–0.75 V respectively, which could be attributed to its wider layer spacing offering a rapid pathway for sodium ion intercalation and removal and the inclusion of CDs creating more edge defect sites, further enhancing the fast energy storage capabilities.

Subsequently, the Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration (GITT) was employed to further study the kinetic properties. The GITT method was utilized to measure the diffusion coefficient of Na+ by applying a pulse current at 0.1C for 30 min, followed by rest intervals of 2 h (Fig. 6g). The diffusion coefficient of Na+ (DNa) could be obtained by Fick's second law and formula 6[8]:

(V) denote the voltage changes resulting from the current pulse and constant current discharge, respectively. The DNa test results of HC and HC/CDs-3 during discharge and charge are depicted in Fig. 6h (the enlarged image at low potential is shown for illustration purposes). Both HC and HC/CDs-3 exhibited similar changes in DNa suggesting that their storage mechanisms are comparable. The DNa in the slope region exhibits a significantly larger size compared to that in the platform region during the sodiation process, which suggests that sodiation primarily occurs in easily accessible locations, such as surface and edge defects[64]. As the adsorption of Na+ on the surface gradually increases, they must overcome the repulsive charge gradient of previously bound Na+, which is also the reason for the gradual decrease DNa aches at a minimum value due to the further increase in diffusion resistance, including Na+ charge repulsion and difficulty in Na+ intercalation. However, an almost U-turn-like reversal occurs at 50 mV, which is attributed to the shift in the sodium storage mechanism from intercalation to filling closed holes[65]. During the desodiation process (Fig. 6i), the diffusion coefficient initially decreases to 0.75 V, then increases, and finally decreases again until reaching the cut-off voltage. In addition, compared with the diffusion coefficient during discharge, the diffusion coefficient increases sharply at 0.2 V due to the Na+ is extracted from the closed pore by repulsive force. In addition the contribution of CDs to sodium ion storage was verified by non-in-situ TEM, as shown in Fig. S14. Obviously, the non-uniform distribution of sodium ions on the surface of HC/CDs-3 is attributed to the fact that sodium ions preferentially absorbed around CDs rich in surface defects.

In order to verify the practicability of HC/CDs-3 electrode materials, a full battery was assembled with layered metal oxide O3-NaFe0.33Ni0.33Mn0.33O (NFNMO) as the cathode and HC/CDs-3 as the anode (Fig. 7 a). At current densities of 0.1C (the nominal specific capacities of the positive and negative materials are 120 mAh g−1 and 300 mAh g−1 respectively), the half-cell of HC/CDs-3 and NFNMO exhibit reversible capacities of 368 and 75 mAh g−1, respectively (Fig. 7b). To optimize the capacity and stability of the full battery, the capacity of cathode is set at 1.2 times that of the anode. Moreover, assembling a full battery at a relatively high N/P ratio ensures that the capacity does not decrease due to excessive use of the anode[66]. The electrochemical performance of the prepared full battery is shown in Fig. 7c and d. At the current density of 0.1C (1C = 300 mAh g−1), the ICE and the reversible capacity are 60 % and 290 mAh g−1 respectively. With the increase of current density, the specific capacity of the full battery could reach 290, 273, 236, 203, 177 and 130 mAh g−1 showing an excellent magnification performance. In addition, at the current density of 0.2C, the capacity retention rate of the full battery could reach 74.5 % after 200 cycles of charge and discharge (Fig. 7e). Based on the combined mass of the positive and negative electrode materials, the energy density of the coin-type full battery was calculated to be 232 Wh kg−1. Furthermore, a single battery was able to illuminate the lamp strip depicted in Fig. 7f, confirming that HC/CDs-3 holds promising potential as a practical negative electrode material for SIBs.

In summary, hard carbon anchoring with CDs was successfully prepared by simple crosslinking, preoxidation and carbonization. Specifically, the agglomeration of CDs at high temperature could be avoided by the preoxidation anchoring strategy, and the quantity of carbon quantum dots and microstructure of hard carbon can be effectively regulated by adjusting the amount of crosslinking agent. Due to the large number of defect sites brought by CDs, the slope capacity of HC/CDs-3 increases significantly, meanwhile the optimization of hard carbon microstructure also enhances the platform capacity of HC/CDs-3. Finally, the collaborative majorization of slope/platform capacity significantly improves the specific capacity, rate performance and cycle stability of HC/CDs-3, representing an excellent comprehensive performance. When used with a NaFe0.33Ni0.33Mn0.33O cathode, the battery has excellent capacity and cycle life, superior to most recently reported negative electrode materials. This study offers insights into the preparation of nanostructured composite hard carbon materials and comprehensively improving the electrochemical performance of sodium ion batteries.
